summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the 'mole conversion map'?

The conversion map places moles in the center. From moles you can go to grams (× molar mass), particles (× Avogadro's number), or liters (× 22.4 at STP).

What is a 'conversion factor'?

A conversion factor expresses a relationship between two units. Multiplying by it changes units without changing the actual amount.

What is '22.4 L/mol' called?

22.4 L/mol is the molar volume, valid only at STP. At other temperatures/pressures, use the ideal gas law PV = nRT.

What is the difference between 'moles to grams' and 'moles to particles'?

Two different conversion factors branch from moles: × molar mass → grams, × 6.022 × 10²³ → particles. They measure different things.

A student is asked to find the mass of 3 moles of CO₂ (molar mass = 44 g/mol). What is the answer?

3 mol × 44 g/mol = 132 g. Multiply moles by molar mass to get grams.

An industrial tank holds 5 mol of chlorine gas at STP. What is the volume?

5 mol × 22.4 L/mol = 112 L. The molar volume applies to all ideal gases, including Cl₂.
